abstract: This article examines the divergent outcomes of principled versus strategic nonviolent movements. While pragmatic, strategic nonviolence has proven effective at toppling corrupt and dictatorial regimes, it often falters when tasked with the deeper transformation of state and political systems. The study argues that this failure stems from a fundamental difference in orientation: principled nonviolent movements maintain a healthy ambivalence toward traditional political power and the coercive mechanisms of the Weberian state. In contrast, strategic movements are frequently willing to harness state power for their own ends—a compromise that has led to fatal results in contexts such as Egypt, Palestine, and Syria. Ultimately, the promise of principled nonviolence lies in its potential to build social, political, and economic institutions that transcend Machiavellian power dynamics through a radical commitment to pacifism and emancipatory processes.
